scenes.title: Sine and Cosine: Building Blocks
scenes.description: The sine and cosine functions are the fundamental building blocks of all periodic signals. They arise naturally from a point rotating on the unit circle.
scenes.markdown:
# Sine and Cosine: The Building Blocks

The unit circle parametrized by angle $t$:

$$x(t) = \cos t, \quad y(t) = \sin t, \quad t \in [0, 2\pi]$$

Projects onto two waves:
- **Horizontal projection** gives the **cosine** wave
- **Vertical projection** gives the **sine** wave

## Key Properties

$$\sin(t + 2\pi) = \sin(t), \quad \cos(t + 2\pi) = \cos(t)$$

Both functions are **periodic** with period $T = 2\pi$.

## Orthogonality

Sine and cosine are **orthogonal** on $[0, 2\pi]$:

$$\int_0^{2\pi} \sin(nt)\cos(mt)\,dt = 0 \quad \text{for all } n, m$$

$$\int_0^{2\pi} \sin(nt)\sin(mt)\,dt = \begin{cases}\pi & n = m \\ 0 & n \neq m\end{cases}$$

This orthogonality is what makes Fourier series work — the basis functions do not interfere with each other.

## Frequency and Amplitude

Generalizing: $A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ has:
- **Amplitude** $A$ — height of the wave
- **Frequency** $\omega$ — oscillations per $2\pi$
- **Phase** $\phi$ — horizontal shift

> **The Circle View**: The rotating point on the unit circle PRODUCES the sine wave as its $y$-coordinate. Watching the circle is the most intuitive way to understand all of trigonometry.

views.name: Iso
views.description: Angled isometric view — see the waveform in 3D perspective

views.name: Top
views.description: Top view looking down — edge-on view of the waveform

views.name: Face On
views.description: Face-on view — primary waveform viewing angle

views.name: Side
views.description: Side view along X — see the amplitude profile

steps.title: The unit circle
steps.description: A point rotating on the unit circle: $x = \cos t$, $y = \sin t$. One full revolution takes $t = 2\pi$.

steps.title: The sine wave
steps.description: The $y$-coordinate of the rotating point traces the **sine wave** $y = \sin(t)$ as $t$ increases from 0 to $2\pi$.

steps.title: The cosine wave
steps.description: The $x$-coordinate traces the **cosine wave** $y = \cos(t)$. Cosine is sine shifted left by $\pi/2$: $\cos(t) = \sin(t + \pi/2)$.

steps.title: Higher frequencies
steps.description: The frequency-2 sine wave $\sin(2t)$ completes 2 full oscillations in one period. These higher harmonics are the building blocks of the Fourier series.

scenes.title: Square Wave Approximation
scenes.description: A square wave can be built from sine waves alone: $f(x) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left(\sin x + \frac{\sin 3x}{3} + \frac{\sin 5x}{5} + \cdots\right)$. More terms means a sharper approximation.
scenes.markdown:
# Fourier Series: Square Wave

The **square wave** with period $2\pi$ and amplitude 1 can be represented as an infinite sum of sine waves:

$$f(x) = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\sin\bigl((2k+1)x\bigr)}{2k+1} = \frac{4}{\pi}\left(\sin x + \frac{\sin 3x}{3} + \frac{\sin 5x}{5} + \cdots\right)$$

## Why Only Odd Harmonics?

The square wave is an **odd function** (symmetric about the origin) and has **half-wave symmetry** (the second half is minus the first half). These symmetries force:
- Cosine coefficients: $a_n = 0$ (odd function)
- Even sine coefficients: $b_{2k} = 0$ (half-wave symmetry)

## Fourier Coefficients

For a general periodic function $f$ with period $2\pi$:

$$a_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\cos(nx)\,dx, \quad b_n = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} f(x)\sin(nx)\,dx$$

For the square wave: $b_n = \frac{4}{n\pi}$ (odd $n$), $0$ (even $n$).

## Gibbs Phenomenon

At the jump discontinuities ($x = 0, \pm\pi$), the partial sums always overshoot by about **8.9%** no matter how many terms are used. This is the **Gibbs phenomenon**.

> **Watch**: Adding each new odd harmonic adds a new "wiggle" while sharpening the approximation at smooth regions.

views.name: Face On
views.description: Wider face-on view — see all frequency components of the square wave

steps.title: Target: the square wave
steps.description: The ideal square wave: $+1$ for $x \in (0, \pi)$ and $-1$ for $x \in (-\pi, 0)$. We will build this from pure sine waves.

steps.title: $N=1$: First term $\frac{4}{\pi}\sin(x)$
steps.description: The first term alone is just a sine wave scaled to $4/\pi \approx 1.27$. It captures the right sign but is very smooth.

steps.title: $N=3$: Three terms through $\sin(5x)/5$
steps.description: $\frac{4}{\pi}(\sin x + \frac{\sin 3x}{3} + \frac{\sin 5x}{5})$. Already much closer to the square wave with flat top and bottom.

steps.title: $N=7$: Seven terms, sharper corners
steps.description: Seven odd harmonics. The flat regions are well-approximated, but small Gibbs overshoots appear near the jumps.

steps.title: Interactive: choose number of terms $N$
steps.description: Drag the $N$ slider to add more odd harmonics. Watch the Gibbs phenomenon at $x = 0, \pm\pi$ — it never fully disappears!

scenes.title: Phasors in 3D: The Helix Visualization
scenes.description: Each Fourier term is a rotating phasor. In 3D — with time as the z-axis — every frequency component traces a helix. The y-z shadow of the combined helix IS the square wave.
scenes.markdown:
# Fourier in 3D: Rotating Phasors

Every Fourier term is a **rotating phasor** — a vector spinning in the complex plane at a fixed frequency. In 3D, we let time advance along the **z-axis**, so each phasor sweeps out a **helix**:

$$\text{Term } n:\quad x(t) = A_n\cos(nt),\quad y(t) = A_n\sin(nt),\quad z = t$$

## Two Views, Two Insights

| Camera | What you see |
|--------|--------------|
| **End On** (along −z) | Phasors as circles — radius = amplitude, speed = frequency |
| **Wave View** (along −x) | The **y-z shadow** of the helix — the actual waveform |

## Square Wave Phasors

Only odd harmonics appear, each with amplitude $4/(n\pi)$:

$$x(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\!\left(\cos t + \frac{\cos 3t}{3} + \frac{\cos 5t}{5} + \cdots\right)$$
$$y(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\!\left(\sin t + \frac{\sin 3t}{3} + \frac{\sin 5t}{5} + \cdots\right)$$

The combined helix grows increasingly complex in 3D — but its **y-z shadow** step by step becomes a square wave.

## The Deep Insight

A Fourier series is not merely algebra — it is a **geometric fact**: any periodic signal is the shadow of rotating helices cast onto a plane.

> **Gibbs phenomenon**: Even with $N=8$ terms the shadow overshoots ~9% at each jump. The overshoot gets thinner but never vanishes no matter how many helices you add.

views.name: End On
views.description: Looking along the time axis — phasors appear as overlapping circles, radius = amplitude

views.name: Wave View
views.description: Side view along x — the y-z shadow on the back wall is the actual waveform

views.name: Iso
views.description: Isometric view — see the full 3D helix structure

views.name: Top
views.description: Top view — see the phasor paths from above

steps.title: A phasor traces a helix in 3D
steps.description: A phasor of amplitude 1 rotating at frequency 1 sweeps through time. In 3D this traces a **helix**. Switch to **End On** to see a circle; switch to **Wave View** to see that its y-shadow is $\sin(t)$.

steps.title: n=1: the fundamental phasor
steps.description: The first square-wave term has amplitude $4/\pi \approx 1.27$, giving a wider helix. Switch to **Wave View** — the dark shadow on the back wall is $(4/\pi)\sin(t)$: a smooth start.

steps.title: n=3: the third harmonic phasor
steps.description: The third harmonic has amplitude $4/(3\pi) \approx 0.42$ and spins **3× faster** — a tighter, narrower helix. From **End On** it appears as a smaller inner circle. Both phasors now rotate simultaneously.

steps.title: N=3 combined: the sum phasor
steps.description: Adding the two phasors gives a winding 3D path. Switch to **Wave View** — the y-shadow is already recognizable as a rough square wave! A complex 3D path, a simple shadow.

steps.title: N=7: the square wave emerges from the shadow
steps.description: Seven phasors. The 3D helix grows intricate — but switch to **Wave View** and the shadow is nearly a perfect square wave. Seven rotating helices conspire to cast a square shadow.

steps.title: Interactive: watch the helix and its shadow
steps.description: Drag $N$ from 1 to 8. Watch the 3D helix path change — and in **Wave View**, the shadow sharpen toward a square wave. Notice the Gibbs overshoot near the jumps never fully disappears.

scenes.title: Why Square Waves Arise: Bistable Dynamics
scenes.description: Square waves emerge naturally from any system with two stable states. A periodic force tilts the energy landscape until one well disappears — the system snaps to the other side. Flat regions are stable equilibrium; sharp jumps are fast barrier crossings.
scenes.markdown:
# Why Square Waves Arise: Bistable Dynamics

Many real systems have **two stable equilibria** — two states they naturally settle into. Under periodic forcing such systems **snap** between states, tracing a square wave in time.

## The Double Well

The simplest model: a particle in a **double-well potential**:

$$V(x) = x^4 - 2x^2$$

Stable minima at $x = \pm 1$, unstable barrier at $x = 0$.

## Periodic Forcing

An external tilt $-A\sin(t)\cdot x$ periodically raises one well:

$$V(x,t) = x^4 - 2x^2 - A\sin(t)\cdot x$$

- $\sin(t) > 0$: right well deepens, left wall shrinks → ball snaps **right**
- $\sin(t) < 0$: left well deepens, right wall shrinks → ball snaps **left**

## Why the Jumps Are Sharp

At the barrier $x=0$, the restoring force $-dV/dx = 4x - 4x^3$ is zero — but once the ball tips past, the cubic term accelerates it rapidly into the other well. The steeper the barrier, the faster the snap, and the **sharper the jump** in the time signal.

## Real-World Examples

| System | State $-1$ | State $+1$ |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| Digital logic | LOW (0 V) | HIGH (5 V) |
| Thermostat | Heater OFF | Heater ON |
| Neuron | Resting | Firing |
| Relay | Open | Closed |

> **Key insight**: Square waves don't require elaborate construction — they arise from any bistable system under periodic forcing. Fourier just tells you what frequencies are hidden inside the shape.

views.name: Potential
views.description: Face-on view of the double-well potential in the x-y plane

views.name: Time View
views.description: Side view from y: position (vertical) vs time (horizontal) — reveals the square wave

views.name: Iso
views.description: Isometric view — see both the potential shape and the 3D trajectory

views.name: Top
views.description: Top view looking down at the trajectory path

steps.title: Two stable states: the double well
steps.description: The potential $V(x) = x^4 - 2x^2$ has **two minima** at $x = \pm 1$ and an unstable **barrier** at $x = 0$. A ball placed anywhere will roll into one of the two valleys and stay. Neither state is preferred — both are equally stable. Switch to **Potential** view for a clear 2D picture.

steps.title: Trapped in equilibrium: a flat signal
steps.description: Without forcing the ball sits at $x = -1$ forever — its position over time is a **flat line**. The z-axis is time; the line extends straight ahead without changing. No wave, just a constant. To get a square wave we need something to periodically push the ball over the barrier.

steps.title: Periodic forcing tilts the landscape
steps.description: The force $-A\sin(t)\cdot x$ periodically tilts the well. Each curve is a snapshot of $V(x)$ at a different time. **Orange** ($t = \pi/2$): right well deepened, left wall nearly gone → ball snaps right. **Bright blue** ($t = 3\pi/2$): the reverse. Switch to **Potential** view to compare the shapes.

steps.title: The ball snaps: a square wave is born
steps.description: The ball's full 3D trajectory through time. It hugs the well bottom at $x = \pm 1$ (travelling along $y \approx -0.7$) and briefly rises to $y = 0$ as it crosses the barrier. Switch to **Time View** to see this is a square wave: long flat stretches at $\pm 1$, sharp vertical jumps at the transitions.
